summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – ENFORCEMENT – Where final orders had been made in 2017 – Where the orders required the applicant to pay the respondent $34,000 within 30 days and for him to transfer to her his right, title and interest in a property – Where contemporaneously with the transfer, the applicant was to release the respondent from his personal covenants to a bank – Where the respondent contends the applicant did not comply with the 2017 orders and that it would now be inequitable to enforce the orders – Where the Court is not persuaded that it would be inequitable to enforce the orders

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

By way of enforcement application filed 24 September 2025, the applicant seeks to enforce orders made by the Federal Circuit Court of Australia on 21 December 2017.
The specific orders that are sought to be enforced are the following:
Within 30 days from the date of this Order, the mother shall pay to the father the sum of $34,000 and the father shall transfer to the mother his right, title and interest in the property situated at [B Street] [suburb C].
Contemporaneously with the transfer in paragraph 2 the mother shall release the father from his personal covenants of the Australia and New Zealand Banking Corporation (‘ANZ’) mortgage.
The application is opposed by the respondent.
On 3 December 2025, Judicial Registrar Gelston made orders for the respondent to file and serve an affidavit in response to the Application – Enforcement by 21 January 2026 or in the alternative a Response or an Initiating Application setting out with precision the orders sought pursuant to s 79A. The respondent filed both an affidavit and on 2 February 2026 an Initiating Application seeking to vary the orders made pursuant to s 79A(1)(c) of the Act.

BACKGROUND

On 21 December 2017, pursuant to Pt VIII of the Act, orders were made by the then Federal Circuit Court of Australia. The orders required the applicant to pay the respondent $34,000 within 30 days and for him to transfer to her his right, title and interest in a property (Order 2). Contemporaneously with the transfer the applicant was to release the respondent from his personal covenants to the ANZ bank (Order 3).
The applicant in her affidavit gives evidence that on 8 January 2018 she deposited into her solicitor’s trust account the sum of $34,000. On 10 January 2018, the applicant’s solicitors wrote to the respondent requesting him to sign a transfer document and recorded in the letter that when he had signed the transfer, he was to return it to their office and that they undertook not to lodge the same until payment had been made of $34,000.
The proposal contained in the letter of 10 January 2018 was repeated in a letter dated 11 January 2018.
By email in reply dated 11 January 2018 at 5.52 pm, the respondent said:
I am not accepting $34,000. I need pay out $90,000 in cash because the property is [worth] over $350,000.
Following that correspondence neither party did anything to give effect to the 2017 orders for nearly 8 years, save that in May 2023 the applicant discharged the loan secured by the bank over the home.
On 11 February 2025, the respondent filed an application for enforcement which application was dismissed by a judge of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia on 12 August 2025.
On 29 August 2025, the applicant’s solicitor again wrote to the respondent seeking his compliance with the orders. The respondent did not respond.
On 1 September 2025, the applicant paid $34,000 to the respondent by depositing the sum into the respondent’s bank account.
On 2 September 2025, the respondent by email to the applicant’s solicitors stating among other things, “I am definitely refusing $34,000”.
On 10 September 2025, the applicant says that a cheque for $34,000 payable to the applicant was delivered to the applicant’s solicitor’s office.
On 2 February 2026, the applicant filed an Initiating Application pursuant to s 79A.

APPLICABLE LAW

Section 105(1) of the Act provides as follows:
Subject to this part, to the regulations, and to the applicable rules of court and all decrees made under this Act may be enforced by any court having jurisdiction under this Act.
In Suris & Suris (No 2) [2023] FedCFamC1F 89 Carew J observed as follows:
In McMillan & McMillan, Tree J provides a helpful overview of the relevant authorities at [37] – [60] and I adopt, with respect, his Honour’s conclusions as to the applicable principles which I summarise as follows (citations omitted):
(a) The nature of the power to enforce is discretionary as evidenced by the use of the term “may” in s 105 of the Act;

(b) Only the facts or circumstances arising since the date of order sought to be enforced will inform the discretion to be exercised pursuant to s 105 of the Act;

(c) The Court may refuse to enforce an order if it would be “inequitable” to do so;

(d) When considering whether or not it would be inequitable to enforce the order, the Court is primarily concerned with “general notions of fairness”;

(e) The party submitting that it would be inequitable to enforce the order bears the onus of so establishing;

(f) Delay in bringing an application for enforcement is a relevant factor.

(Footnotes omitted)

DISCUSSION

Adopting her Honour’s useful summary of the law, I accept that the exercise of power to enforce orders of the Court are discretionary and the Court may refuse to enforce an order if it would be inequitable to do so.
The respondent bears the onus of establishing to the satisfaction of the Court that it would be inequitable to enforce the orders.
The respondent contends that the Court would not enforce the orders because the applicant did not comply with the 2017 orders by not paying the respondent the sum of $34,000 and as best I understand his argument, as a consequence of her default it would now be inequitable, given the passing of time and what he says is an increase in the value of the property, to enforce the orders.
His counsel submits in the Case Outline as follows:
The Applicant was unable to refinance the [suburb C] property within 30 days of the Orders made on 21 December 2017 (Orders 2 and 3) and did not release the Respondent from his personal covenants with the ANZ mortgage as she was unable to do so.
The Applicant’s solicitor provided no follow up correspondence to the correspondence dated 11 January 2018 specific to the releasing the Respondent from his personal covenants with the ANZ mortgage as she was unable to refinance the property.
The Applicant was in receipt of Centrelink income and child support and was unable to refinance the [suburb C] property in compliance with the 2017 Orders.
Despite paying out the mortgage on 8 May 2023 the Applicant had not released the Respondent from his personal covenant with the ANZ mortgage in compliance with the 2017 Orders.
The Applicant sought refund of the balance of the $34,000.00 she had deposited into her solicitor trust account as she was unable to refinance the [suburb C] property.
The Applicant’s failure to comply with the 2017 Orders should have invoked the implementation of Order 4, requiring the property to be sold upon default in January 2018. (See Goldsmith & Stinson (No 2) [2023] FedCFamC1A 25 and the authorities discussed there.)
Further, no order should be made at this time without first hearing the s 79A application of the Respondent.
I am not persuaded that it would be inequitable to enforce the orders. I reach that conclusion for the following reasons:
(1) I am satisfied that the applicant held funds in her solicitors trust account sufficient to make the payment to the respondent in the time required by the orders.

(2) The applicant’s solicitors wrote to the respondent (who was unrepresented) requesting that he sign a transfer to enable Order 2 to be implemented.

(3) The respondent did not sign the transfer.

(4) At a time prior to the expiry of the period in which the applicant was required to make the payment the respondent made clear that he would not accept the payment of $34,000 thereby frustrating the applicant’s compliance with the orders.

(5) The applicant’s obligation to comply with Order 3 arouse upon and contemporaneously with the respondent’s compliance with Order 2 which he in clear and unequivocal terms made plain he would not comply.

(6) There is no evidence that provides a foundation for the matters contained at paragraphs 3 and 5 of the respondent’s Case Outline nor any evidence that the applicant was “unable to refinance the property” as asserted in paragraph 4 of the respondent’s Case Outline.

(7) It would be inequitable to permit the respondent to rely upon his own default as a basis for which to exercise the discretion not to enforce. To do so would be to reward his failure to comply with the order and inconsistent with the obligations imposed on those who approach a court seeking an equitable remedy.

(8) There is no admissible evidence that the property is valued at $700,000. The respondent does not set out the basis for his assertion that the property has a value of $700,000 and his unqualified opinion carries no weight.

(9) I am not satisfied that the observations, ratio or dicta of the Full Court in Goldsmith & Stinson (No 2) [2023] FedCFamC1A 25 bears in any way on the determination of this application which arises entirely as a consequence of the actions of the respondent and does not prejudice the respondent nor compromise the relief sought by him in his Initiating Application.

DISPOSITION

For the above reasons, I propose to make the orders as sought by the respondent.
I will make a further order that the applicant is to send by pre-paid post to the respondent’s solicitors, the bank cheque held in her solicitor’s possession. It is a matter for the respondent to do with the cheque as he sees fit.

COSTS

The applicant seeks an order that the respondent pay her costs of and incidental to the application in the sum of $1,964. The respondent’s counsel quite properly concedes an order for costs.
An order will be made that the respondent pay the applicant’s costs in the sum sought.
